story_title: The Witwatersrand Discovery — The Richest Gold Reef on Earth
summary: When Australian prospector George Harrison uncovered gold-bearing reef on Langlaagte farm on the Witwatersrand ridge in February 1886, he unknowingly found the largest gold deposit in human history: the Witwatersrand Basin has yielded over 50,000 tonnes of gold — roughly 40 percent of all the gold ever mined — and created Johannesburg from a barren highveld farm within four years.
recommended_arc: Hook: a lone prospector cracks open a rock and finds a gold-bearing reef stretching 500 km. Rising: the biggest mining houses in the world descend on a barren Boer republic. Climax: Johannesburg founded in 1886 — a city of 100,000 in four years. Payoff: the Rand drives 40 percent of all gold ever mined; it also ignites the Second Boer War.

verified_facts.claim: George Harrison, an Australian prospector, is credited with discovering the main Witwatersrand gold reef on Langlaagte farm near present-day Johannesburg in February or March 1886; the Transvaal government declared the area a public goldfield on 20 September 1886.

The Witwatersrand (Afrikaans: 'Ridge of White Waters') gold deposits are part of a sedimentary basin stretching approximately 400 kilometres in an arc across what is now South Africa's Gauteng and North West provinces.
The Witwatersrand Basin has produced an estimated 50,000 tonnes of gold since 1886, accounting for approximately 40 percent of all the gold ever mined by humanity.

Johannesburg was established as a township on 4 October 1886 by the Transvaal government to service the gold rush; by 1890 it had grown from a camp into a settlement of approximately 100,000 people and by 1900 had a population of over 150,000.

By 1898 the Transvaal — the Boer republic containing the Witwatersrand — was producing approximately 27 percent of the world's entire gold output; the conflict over control of this wealth was a primary cause of the Second Boer War (1899-1902).

South Africa remained the world's largest gold producer every year from 1905 until 2007, when it was surpassed by China; at its peak in 1970 South Africa produced approximately 1,000 tonnes of gold per year — roughly 80 percent of the world's total output.
